label: Modding TL;DR
title: A quick TL;DR on Unity, C#, Decompilation, and BepInEx
lead: To create a BepInEx plugin, we must first create the universe...
bodyHtml:
The Unity mental model

Casualties Unknown is a Unity game. That means the running game is mostly a collection of loaded Scene objects, each containing GameObject objects. A GameObject is just a container until you attach Component objects to it.

Every GameObject has a Transform, which stores position, rotation, scale, and parent/child hierarchy. Most visible things in the game are GameObjects with components such as renderers, colliders, UI scripts, inventory scripts, or custom gameplay scripts.

GameObject player = PlayerCamera.main.body.gameObject;
Transform playerTransform = player.transform;
Vector3 position = playerTransform.position;

Scripts are components

A normal Unity gameplay script usually inherits from MonoBehaviour. MonoBehaviours are components, so they live on GameObjects and receive Unity lifecycle methods like Awake, Start, and Update.

In a mod, you do not usually create a Unity project and press Play. You compile C# into a DLL, BepInEx loads that DLL into the already-running game, and your code interacts with Unity objects the game created.

What is a MonoBehaviour?

public sealed class SimpleUnityExample : MonoBehaviour
{
    private void Awake() { }
    private void Start() { }
    private void Update() { }
}

Lifecycle basics

Awake runs when Unity creates the object. Start runs before the first frame update, once the component is enabled. Update runs every rendered frame. FixedUpdate runs on a fixed timestep and is mostly for physics-style work.

For modding, this matters because not every game object exists when your plugin loads. Plugin Awake is great for registering content, config, and patches. World/player logic often needs to wait until the gameplay scene exists.

private void Update()
{
    elapsed += Time.deltaTime;
}

Time, frames, and why Update is expensive

Unity games do not run code once and stop. Frame methods run constantly. If a mod does slow searches, reflection, file reads, or allocations in Update, the cost repeats every frame.

I would know, I had 17 bug reports for QoL: Unknown due to this...

Time.deltaTime is the time in seconds since the previous frame. If code means "one unit per second" instead of "one unit per frame", multiply by deltaTime.

// Good shape for a timer in Update:
timer += Time.deltaTime;
if (timer > 1f)
{
    timer = 0f;
}

Prefabs and assets

A Prefab is a reusable GameObject template. In normal Unity development, prefabs live in the project. In modding, you are usually reading, cloning, or referencing prefabs and assets that already exist in the compiled game.

Making a new prefab from scratch is very hard without the Unity editor.

For Casualties Unknown, decompiled code and runtime inspection help you learn what prefabs, components, fields, and method names the game already uses.

Mod code usually instantiates or modifies runtime objects. It does not edit the original Unity project prefab.


// The following code makes a UnityEngine Object in the game world, using the prefab "glowplantfruit" as a template.

Instantiate(Resources.Load("glowplantfruit"), transform.position, transform.rotation);

/*
Resources.Load("glowplantfruit") -> Loads prefab
Instantiate(prefab, position, rotation) -> Clones prefab into the world as a GameObject which then can be interacted with.
*/

Decompiling for values

Use dnSpyEx when you need to inspect C# code values, field names, method names, item IDs, or how an existing mechanic works. You are reading the compiled game code as reference. Do not edit the game DLL.

The file is at:

C:\Program Files (x86)\Steam\steamapps\common\Casualties Unknown Playtest\CasualtiesUnknown_Data\Managed\Assembly-CSharp.dll

Open Assembly-CSharp.dll in dnSpyEx. That is where all of the game's scripts live.

The decompiled code showing CoilScript.cs's Shock() method. Ouch!

dnSpyEx searches worth learning

Ctrl + Shift + K opens the search window. Search "All of the Above" for function/class names, or "Number/String" when you know a literal value shown in-game. For example, searching loot amount can lead you toward PreRunScript.

Ctrl + Shift + R analyzes references. If you are reading Body.cs and find a field like thirst, reference analysis helps find everything that reads or changes it.

Important scripts
  • Item.cs: item list and item properties.
  • Body.cs: movement, actions, and most body/player variables.
  • Liquids.cs: liquids and their effects.

Extracting images, sounds, and assets

Use AssetRipper when you need the game's textures, sounds, sprites, or other Unity assets as reference material.

Run the AssetRipper GUI, select your installed Casualties Unknown folder, and extract all content. For this workflow, do not extract as a Unity project.

After extraction, browse the output folders. Images are commonly under Texture2D, and sounds are commonly under Sounds.

How BepInEx fits in

A BepInEx mod is a compiled C# DLL placed under BepInEx/plugins. BepInEx loads that DLL into the game process. Your plugin entry class inherits from BaseUnityPlugin, which gives you Unity lifecycle behavior plus BepInEx helpers like Logger.

CUCoreLib is a BepInEx plugin dependency. It gives you shared registration APIs and runtime helpers, but it does not change how you write your plugin or scripts.

Recommended references

  • Unity MonoBehaviour scripting API
  • Unity Manual: Prefabs
  • BepInEx: Writing a basic plugin
